title: Painting air-dry clay with acrylics
subtitle: Applying acrylic paint to bone-dry clay for colour and surface finish
excerpt: Painting bone-dry air-dry clay with acrylics is the most accessible way to add colour. Acrylics dry fast, bond well to unglazed clay, and can be layered. The painted surface needs a sealing coat of diluted PVA or acrylic varnish to protect it from handling. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Acrylic painting on clay technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: clay because both surfaces are porous and the acrylic fills the surface pores as it dries. Painting before the clay is fully dry causes the paint to crack and peel as the clay shrinks. Wait until the clay is completely white in colour throughout, not just on the surface, and cool rather than slightly warm to the touch.

attrs.body: A thin base coat of PVA diluted 1:3 with water applied before painting seals the clay surface and reduces the amount of paint absorbed on the first coat, giving better coverage. Always seal the finished painted piece with a further two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 or a dedicated acrylic varnish to protect the paint from handling.
attrs.title: Seal before and after

items.name: PVA diluted 1:3 for base coat and 1:1 for sealing
Wider soft brush for base coat (pottery brush, mop)

content.text: Apply one thin coat of PVA diluted 1:3 with water over the whole piece. Allow to dry fully, this takes five to ten minutes. The surface will be slightly shiny when dry. This base coat reduces absorption and gives the first paint coat better coverage.

Apply the background colour in two thin coats, allowing each to dry before adding the next. Thin the paint slightly with water so it flows into textured areas without leaving brush marks. Paint the whole piece in the base colour before adding detail, it is easier to paint over a single colour than to fill gaps around detailed work.
Step 3: Dry-brush highlights on textured surfaces

is particularly effective on textured clay. Load a stiff brush with a light-coloured paint, wipe most of it off on a cloth, then drag the brush across the raised texture. Paint deposits on the peaks of the texture and leaves the recesses in the dark base colour. This reads as depth and amplifies impressed or relief decoration.

Add fine lines and detail with the liner brush and a slightly thickened paint. Thin paint runs on clay surfaces. For geometric lines, a strip of low-tack masking tape placed on a dry painted surface gives a clean edge.

Once the paint is dry (at least an hour after the final coat), apply two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water, or an acrylic varnish in matte or gloss finish. The varnish protects the paint from handling. Do not use the piece in contact with water or food without a waterproof varnish.

attrs.intro: Problems when painting air-dry clay with acrylics.

items.fix: Allow the clay to dry for at least 48 hours before painting, or test by holding it against a cool cheek, if it feels cool at all, it is still damp. Reapply the base coat before repainting.
items.cause: Painted before the clay was fully bone-dry, or no base coat applied.

items.fix: Apply the PVA base coat, allow to dry, then apply two thin coats of the base colour. The second coat evens out the blotchiness.
items.cause: No sealant base coat applied before painting.
items.symptom: First paint coat soaks in unevenly and looks blotchy

attrs.heading: Common problems
